title: Track your hours for one day. The truth will surprise you.
body: Most of us could not accurately say where the last 24 hours went. The gap between estimated and actual is usually two to four hours per day — spent in scrolling, transitioning, decision-fatigue, or doing nothing on purpose. The audit alone changes behaviour.
reflectionQuestion: If you tracked today honestly, where would the surprise be?

title: Constant input is expensive even when free.
body: Newsletters, feeds, podcasts, video — your attention is finite, and the cost of consuming someone else's thoughts is fewer of your own. The skill of the next decade isn't finding information; it's defending your interior from it.
reflectionQuestion: What's one source of constant input you'd be better off without?

title: Multitasking is task-switching with extra steps.
body: The brain doesn't actually do two things at once — it switches rapidly between them, paying a tax on each switch. Doing one thing for 30 minutes beats doing three things for 30 minutes, in measurable cognitive performance.
reflectionQuestion: When did you last do one thing for 30 minutes uninterrupted?

title: Flow is not the same as busy.
body: Flow is absorbed, energising, restorative. Busy is fragmented, exhausting, depleting. You can tell the difference from how you feel afterwards. Most modern "productivity" is busy disguised as flow.
reflectionQuestion: When did you last experience real flow — and what conditions made it possible?

title: Savouring is the skill of letting good things land.
body: Most people are present for problems and absent for pleasures. Reversing this — staying with a good moment for 15 seconds instead of moving on — rewires the bias your brain has toward negativity.
reflectionQuestion: What pleasure today could you give 15 extra seconds to?

title: Rest is part of the work, not the reward for it.
body: Treating rest as something you earn after sufficient productivity guarantees burnout. The body and mind require rest to function — same as they require sleep. Resting is performing maintenance on the instrument you're using to perform.
reflectionQuestion: What rest have you been postponing until you've "earned it"?

title: Half your yeses are accidents.
body: You said yes because the moment was awkward. Because you didn't want to disappoint. Because the request was small and now it's big. Auditing your yeses is the move that reclaims time fastest.
reflectionQuestion: What's currently in your week that you said yes to by accident?

title: A weekly review is not a to-do list.
body: What went well? What went badly? What did I learn? What do I need to set down? What do I need to pick up? Five questions, fifteen minutes, once a week. More useful than thirty meetings.
reflectionQuestion: What's the most useful question in your weekly review — and are you actually asking it?

title: What would you actually do with one honest hour?
body: Most people, asked this, default to admin or self-improvement. Both miss it. The honest hour is the one you'd spend on the thing you've been wanting to but pretending not to: the call, the writing, the rest, the question.
reflectionQuestion: If you had one honest hour this week, what would it actually go to?

title: Time is the one thing you can't earn back.
body: Money you lose can be re-made. Skills you lose can be re-learned. Relationships you damage can sometimes be repaired. Time spent is gone. Knowing this should make you cheap with yes and generous with the things that matter.
reflectionQuestion: What are you currently giving time to that you wouldn't if you really felt this?

title: What you pay attention to is your life. Literally.
body: Not metaphorically. The texture of your week is whatever your attention was on. If you spent it on a feed, your week was a feed. If you spent it on the people in your house, your week was your people. The list is not flattering for most of us.
reflectionQuestion: What did your attention spend most of this week on — and is that the life you want to be living?

title: You have about 4,000 weeks.
body: If you live to 80. Some of them are done. Most of the rest are spoken for in ways you didn't choose. Realising this is uncomfortable. It's also the most clarifying frame in this entire course.
reflectionQuestion: How many weeks have you spent on each of the things you say matter most?
